text_blocks.snippet: If you are experiencing high levels of stress in Vancouver, there are numerous immediate crisis resources, free, or low-cost services available.

list.snippet: Call or text 9-8-8: The National Suicide Crisis Helpline, available 24/7 for anyone in distress.
310-6789 (no area code needed): BC Mental Health Support Line for emotional support and resources.
Emergency: Call 9-1-1 or visit the nearest emergency department if you are in immediate danger.
BounceBack BC: A free, evidence-based program offering telephone coaching and online tools to help adults and youth (15+) manage stress, anxiety, or low mood.
YMCA Mental Wellness Programs: Free, 7-week, evidence-based programs (Y Mind) for teens (13-18) and young adults (18-30) experiencing mild-to-moderate stress or anxiety.
CMHA North and West Vancouver: Offers short-term, low-cost individual counselling (up to 8 sessions) for adults.
Here to Help: Offers online resources and wellness modules to understand and manage stress.
8-1-1 (HealthLink BC): Call to speak with a nurse for free, non-emergency health information or to find local mental health services.
Vancouver Coastal Health (VCH): Provides mental health and substance use outpatient services, including rapid access clinics.
Access and Assessment Centre (Vancouver): For residents needing mental health support, available for walk-ins or phone calls.
Indigenous Support: KUU-US Crisis Response Service (1-800-588-8717) or Hope for Wellness (1-855-242-3310).
Newcomers/Immigrants: MOSAIC in Vancouver or the Multicultural Mental Health Resource Centre.
Students (UBC): Counselling Services offer virtual/in-person appointments for students.
Group Drumming: Held at Champlain Heights Community Centre for stress reduction.
Sound Bath Meditation: Available at Mount Seymour United Church or St. Paul's Anglican Church.
Nature & Yoga: Winter yoga at Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Classical Chinese Garden.
Walking Therapy: Offered by CMHA North and West Vancouver to combine walking with counselling.

text_blocks.snippet: For non-emergency, professional help, you can also consult your family doctor or a nurse practitioner for a referral.
